opportunities.rationale: Highest-conviction pair this week. LLY continues to execute in obesity (tirzepatide Phase 3 data expected) while NVO has structurally repriced on 2026 guidance cuts and Cagrisema Phase 3 miss. The quality-vs-reset dynamic is intact and insulated from index direction in a high-VIX regime. WTI above $98 adds no direct headwind to LLY; NVO faces currency drag from a weak EUR. Tighten risk to 2%.

Iran strait closure drives WTI past $98; Goldman Sachs Brent target raised to $110 for March. XOM benefits directly from higher crude realisations and Permian volume; UAL faces acute jet fuel cost pressure — fuel is ~25% of airline operating costs. Wells Fargo raised XOM PT to $183. Thesis invalidated on a verified ceasefire or sharp OPEC supply response that normalises Brent below $85.

Secondary energy-vs-airlines pair. Alaska Air is more exposed than majors to leisure routes and does not hedge fuel as aggressively as Delta. Long XOM / short ALK is a cleaner expression of the oil-shock theme with lower correlation to the primary XOM/UAL trade. Exit on confirmed Hormuz de-escalation.

Both names sold off on AI capex fears, but Amazon's multi-year $200B+ capex guide remains far more aggressive than Microsoft's more disciplined Azure path. MSFT has lower energy-cost sensitivity (software margin), while AMZN's AWS and logistics businesses face energy-driven cost inflation. Long MSFT / short AMZN captures quality-of-earnings divergence; risk is a joint rally on broad AI sentiment recovery.

Costco's membership-model resilience and higher-income customer base should allow outperformance vs Walmart in a stagflation environment. WMT faces supply chain pressure from tariffs and a stretched valuation at 30x forward earnings. COST at ~28x is arguably cheaper on a quality-adjusted basis. Long-short keeps beta neutral to broader consumer.

Safe-haven gold miner vs high-beta cyclical consumer. Gold surged to $5,183/oz and Newmont's all-in sustaining costs are fixed; Carnival faces dual pressure from higher fuel costs (fuel ~8% of COGS) and consumer confidence erosion in a war/stagflation environment. CCL earnings are on March 19 — thesis captures the pre-earnings risk premium on the short; hold through print or exit short before close on March 18.

BTC outperformed all major risk assets last week (+6%) with $767M in ETF inflows and Strategy's $776M purchase, while SOL remains a high-beta alt stuck in the risk-off rotation. BTC dominance is rising. Long BTC / short SOL isolates the relative-value dynamic while keeping broad crypto direction-neutral. Size at 1% given crypto vol.

Iran war escalation is the most direct catalyst for defense outperformance. LMT is the primary beneficiary of PAC-3 MSE air defense demand; a recently awarded $9.8B contract with production ramping to 2,000 units/year confirms the earnings runway. Long LMT / short CCL captures war-regime winners vs cyclical consumer losers in a single pair with low index beta.

Gold at $5,183/oz is in a sustained uptrend driven by geopolitical safe-haven demand and central bank accumulation. The S&P is under dual pressure from oil-driven inflation and FOMC uncertainty. Long GLD / short SPY is a macro regime-spread trade: in a stagflation scenario gold's real-value support holds while equities de-rate. Entry on a weekly open dip; exit on a clear risk-on FOMC pivot.

Complementary to the XOM/UAL and XOM/ALK positions — diversifies the energy-vs-airlines theme across two oil majors and three carriers. Chevron is a higher-dividend name with Gulf of Mexico exposure less tied to QatarEnergy disruption risk than XOM. Delta has one of the largest un-hedged fuel exposure books among US carriers and trades at a premium valuation that is vulnerable to guidance cuts.
